No.353/35/2022-NT Government of India Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (Hydrogen Division) 
Atal Akshay Urja Bhawan Opp. CGO Complex, Lodhi Road   New Delhi-110003                   Date: 27th February 2026 
Subject: Green Ammonia Standard for India- Reg, 
The Government of India has approved the National Green Hydrogen 
Mission. For the purpose of the mission and its ensuing policies and 
programmes, following are specified: 
2. "Green Ammonia" means ammonia (NH3) produced using Green 
Hydrogen. In the process of Green Ammonia production, renewable energy also 
includes such electricity generated from renewable sources which is stored in 
an energy storage system or banked with the grid in accordance with applicable 
regulations. 
The total non-biogenic greenhouse gas emissions arising from the 
production of Green Hydrogen, ammonia synthesis, purification, 
compression, and on-site storage shall not exceed 0.38 kilogram of 
carbon dioxide equivalent per kilogram of ammonia (kg CO2 eq/kg 
NH3), taken as an average over last 12-month period. 
3. A detailed methodology for measurement, reporting, monitoring, onsite 
verification, and certification of Green Ammonia shall be specified by the 
Ministry of New and Renewable Energy. 
4. "Green Hydrogen" means hydrogen as defined under the Green Hydrogen 
Standard for India notified by the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy 
(MNRE) vide Office Memorandum No. 353/35/2022-NT dated 18 August 2023, 
as may be amended from time to time. 
Continue.... 
1/2

5. Any tender, bid process, or solicitation issued prior to the date of this 
Notification may continue to be governed by the terms and conditions 
applicable at the time of its issuance; however, the procuring entity may, 
where feasible and with mutual consent of the parties, align such tender 
with the provisions of this Notification. 
6. This issues with the approval of competent authority. 
 
(Prasad A Chaphekar) 
Director 
Email: prasad.chaphekar@gov.in  
To, 
1. All Green Hydrogen Producers 2. All Industry Associations in the field of Renewable Energy

Ministry of New and Renewable Energy 
(Hydrogen Division) 
Atal Akshay Urja Bhawan 
Opp. CGO Complex, Lodhi 
Road, New Delhi-110003 
Date: 27th February 2026 
Subject: Green Methanol Standard for India- Reg. 
The Government of India has approved the National Green Hydrogen Mission. For the purpose of the mission and its ensuing policies and programmes, following are specified: 
2. "Green Methanol" means Methanol (CH3OH) produced using Green Hydrogen. In the process of Green Methanol production, renewable energy also includes such electricity generated from renewable sources which is stored in an energy storage system or banked with the grid in accordance with applicable regulations. 
The total non-biogenic greenhouse gas emissions arising from Green Hydrogen production, methanol synthesis, purification, and on-site storage shall not exceed 0.44 kilogram of carbon dioxide equivalent per kilogram of methanol (kg CO2 eq/kg CH3OH), calculated as an average over the preceding 12-month period. 
3. In the Green Methanol synthesis process, carbon dioxide may be sourced from biogenic sources, Direct Air Capture (DAC), or existing industrial sources. However, the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) may revise, from time to time, the eligible sources of carbon dioxide for Green Methanol production. Any such revision shall apply prospectively, with appropriate grandfathering provisions as determined by MNRE. 
4. A detailed methodology for measurement, reporting, monitoring, onsite verification, and certification of Green Methanol shall be  specified by the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy. 
5. "Green Hydrogen" means hydrogen as defined under the Green Hydrogen Standard for India notified by the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) vide Office Memorandum No. 353/35/2022-NT dated 18 August 2023, as may be amended from time to time. 
Continue....

6. Any tender, bid process, or solicitation issued prior to the date of this Notification may continue to be governed by the terms and conditions applicable at the time of its issuance; however, the procuring entity may, where feasible and with mutual consent of the parties, align such tender with the provisions of this Notification. 
7. This issues with the approval of competent authority. 
 
(Prasad A Chaphekar)

INDIA CELLULAR & ELECTRONICS ASSOCIATION (ICEA)  
India's Post-1991 Industrial and manufacturing Policy Landscape Page 1 of 11 
ASSIGNMENT 
 
 
 
 
Submitted By: 
SHUBHAM KUMAR VERMA 
Master’s in Public Policy, Law, and Governance (2024–2026) 
Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 
Email: shubhamkverma08@gmail.com | shubhamverma.24@kgpian.iitkgp.ac.in 
 
Submitted To: 
India Cellular & Electronics Association (ICEA) 
7th Floor, Le Meridien Commercial Tower 
Windsor Place, Raisina Road, New Delhi - 110001 
Office:  +91-11-49349900

INDIA CELLULAR & ELECTRONICS ASSOCIATION (ICEA)  
India's Post-1991 Industrial and manufacturing Policy Landscape Page 2 of 11 
1.  Why Has Manufacturing Remained at 13–15% of GDP? 
Four interrelated structural factors explain the persistent stagnation of India's manufacturing sector at 13-
15%1 of Gross Domestic Product (GDP). Among them, weak integration into global value chains (GVCs) 
remains the most binding constraint, as even when India attracts investment, as seen under the PLI scheme, 
production often remains shallow and import-dependent. This limited participation in GVCs is itself the 
result of structural and policy constraints, particularly high infrastructure costs, such as unreliable power 
supply and elevated logistics costs, which reduce the competitiveness of Indian firms relative to East Asian 
economies and discourage multinational firms from locating production stages in India.  
 
 
Figure 1: Manufacturing as a % of GDP, India vs. China vs. Vietnam (1991–2024).  
Source: Compilation from World Bank Development Indicators (https://databank.worldbank.org/source/world-development-
indicators) 
1.1 Structural Shift Toward Services 
India's post-liberalisation development was shaped by rapid services sector expansion in IT, 
telecommunications, and finance, which became primary GDP drivers. India leapfrogged manufacturing-led 
growth, leaving services at over 55% of GDP2 and manufacturing structurally under-resourced. 
1.2 Fragmented Industrial Structure 
India’s manufacturing sector exhibits a dualistic structure. The organised (formal) sector accounts for 80% 
of manufacturing output, while the unorganised (informal) sector accounts for over 80% and 99% of Indian 
 
1 “Manufacturing, value added (% of GDP),” Data set, World Bank Open Data (World Bank Group, 2025), 
https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/NV.IND.MANF.ZS?locations=IN. 
2 Ministry of Finance, “SERVICES SECTOR CONTINUES TO CONTRIBUTE SIGNIFICANTLY TO INDIA’S GROWTH, ACCOUNTS FOR 
ABOUT 55 PER CENT OF TOTAL SIZE OF THE ECONOMY IN FY24,” Press release, July 22, 2024. 
https://www.pib.gov.in/PressReleasePage.aspx?PRID=2034920&reg=3&lang=2

INDIA CELLULAR & ELECTRONICS ASSOCIATION (ICEA)  
India's Post-1991 Industrial and manufacturing Policy Landscape Page 3 of 11 
manufacturing employment and establishments3, respectively, highlighting the dominance of small, low-
productivity enterprises. 
1.3 Weak Integration into Global Value Chains 
India's weak GVC participation, reflected in a low GVC Participation Index relative to peers, constrains export 
diversification and productivity gains.4 
 
 
Figure 2: GVC Participation Index, Select Asian Economies (2023).  
 
1.4 Lack of Coordination Across Policies 
Effective industrial policy requires coordination across multiple policy domains. In India, inconsistencies 
between trade and industrial policy, such as inverted tariff structures where tariffs on inputs exceed those 
on final products, have reduced manufacturing competitiveness. Earlier product reservation policies for 
small-scale industries also encouraged firms to specialise in final goods rather than intermediate 
components, limiting their integration into GVCs. 
 
Together, these coordination failures have made it structurally difficult for Indian firms to deepen their 
participation in global production networks 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3 Ejaz Ghani, William Kerr, and Alex Segura, “Rise of Informality in India’s Tradable Manufacturing Sector,” Ideas for India I4I, July 
13, 2015. https://www.ideasforindia.in/topics/macroeconomics/rise-of-informality-in-indias-tradable-manufacturing-sector  
4 Isha Chawla and Nagesh Kumar, “FDI, International Trade and Global Value Chains (GVCs): India’s GVC Participation, Position and 
Value Capture,” Asia and the Global Economy 3, no. 2 (July 1, 2023): 100071, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aglobe.2023.100071.

INDIA CELLULAR & ELECTRONICS ASSOCIATION (ICEA)  
India's Post-1991 Industrial and manufacturing Policy Landscape Page 4 of 11 
2. Policy Evaluation 
2.1 Make in India Initiative (2014–Present) 
Theory of Change 
Launched in September 2014, Make in India aimed to transform India into a global manufacturing hub by 
improving ease of doing business, attracting Foreign Direct Investment (FDI), and signalling a renewed policy 
commitment to industrialisation. The initiative targeted sectors, including defence manufacturing, aviation, 
and electronics, on the assumption that regulatory reforms and investment promotion would catalyse 
manufacturing expansion and employment generation.5 
Where It Succeeded 
• FDI growth: FDI inflows rose from $45.14 billion in 2014–15 to a record $84.83 billion in 2021–22, 
with $667.41 billion received between 2014 and 2024, reflecting growing global confidence in India 
as a manufacturing destination.6 
• Regulatory modernisation: The initiative accelerated digitisation of licensing, simplified 
environmental clearances, and improved India's Ease of Doing Business ranking from 142nd (2014) 
to 63rd (2020).7 
• Policy signalling: It established manufacturing as a national policy priority, laying the institutional 
foundation for more targeted programs including PLI. 
 
 
Figure 4: India FDI Inflows (FY2014–FY2024).  
Source: Compiled from DPIIT, FDI Statistics 2024. 
 
5 R. Nagaraj, “Make in India: Why Didn’t the Lion Roar?,” Ideas for India I4I, May 8, 2019. 
https://www.ideasforindia.in/topics/macroeconomics/make-in-india-why-didnt-the-lion-roar? 
6 Ministry of Commerce & Industry, “10 Years of Make in India,” PIB (Research Unit, Press Information Bureau, September 25, 
2024). https://static.pib.gov.in/WriteReadData/specificdocs/documents/2024/sep/doc2024925401801.pdf.  
7 Ministry of Commerce & Industry, “DPIIT Coordinates Initiatives for Ease of Doing Business Creating a Conducive Business 
Environment,” PIB (Press Information Bureau, February 7, 2024). 
https://www.pib.gov.in/PressReleaseIframePage.aspx?PRID=2003540&reg=3&lang=2.

INDIA CELLULAR & ELECTRONICS ASSOCIATION (ICEA)  
India's Post-1991 Industrial and manufacturing Policy Landscape Page 5 of 11 
Where It Fell Short 
• No Structural Shift in Manufacturing Share: Despite improved FDI metrics, India's manufacturing 
share of GDP did not increase during the Make in India decade, remaining stuck at 13-15%. This 
reveals a fundamental gap between investment attraction and industrial transformation. FDI largely 
flowed into services, real estate, and assembly operations rather than into deep manufacturing with 
backward linkages. 
• Demand-Side Neglect: The initiative prioritised supply-side investment promotion, easing 
regulations and boosting investor confidence, while neglecting key demand-side conditions 
necessary for sustained manufacturing growth. It did not adequately strengthen skill development, 
build procurement linkages between large firms and MSMEs, or deepen domestic supplier 
ecosystems. As a result, this supply-side bias limited its effectiveness in strengthening 
manufacturing competitiveness, a concern also highlighted by ICEA in the context of electronics 
localisation.8 
• Weak Integration into Global Value Chains: Although India attracted investment in sectors such as 
electronics, it remains less integrated into global manufacturing supply chains than countries like 
Vietnam and China. High logistics costs, port inefficiencies, and policy uncertainties have reduced 
export competitiveness. Unlike Vietnam, which complemented industrial policy with export zones 
and trade agreements, India lacked a parallel strategy to scale exports. 
2.2 Production Linked Incentive (PLI) Scheme (2020-Present) 
Theory of Change 
Introduced in March 2020, the PLI scheme provides performance-based financial incentives on incremental 
production and sales to firms across strategic sectors, including electronics, pharmaceuticals, automobiles, 
and renewable energy. The underlying theory of change is that output-linked subsidies will attract global 
manufacturers, creating economies of scale, stimulate domestic production, and progressively integrate 
India into GVCs, marking a shift from passive investment promotion toward active industrial targeting. 
Where It Succeeded 
• Electronics & Smartphones: Mobile phone production rose by around 146%9 as major global 
smartphone companies expanded or shifted production to India, driven by PLI incentives and supply 
chain diversification away from China.  
• Investment mobilisation: A large share of investments under the PLI scheme has been concentrated 
in the mobile phone and IT hardware sectors, which together received more than half of the 
scheme’s allocations during the first three years.10 The success was driven by clear sector-specific 
 
8 “THE CHALLENGES & OPPORTUNITIES IN INDIAN SEMICONDUCTOR INDUSTRY,” ICEA Knowledge Hub (India Cellular and 
Electronics Association), https://icea.org.in/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/0.1-The-challenges-and-opportunities-in-Indian-
Semiconductor-Industry.pdf.  
9 Ministry of Commerce & Industry, “Production Linked Incentive Scheme With ₹1.91 Lakh Crore Outlay Drives Strong Industry 
Participation Across 14 Strategic Sectors,” Press Information Bureau, February 20, 2026. 
https://www.pib.gov.in/PressReleasePage.aspx?PRID=2230621&reg=3&lang=1  
10 Biswajit Dhar, “PLI: On the Way to Another Failed Incentive Scheme?,” The India Forum, January 19, 2024, 
https://www.theindiaforum.in/public-policy/pli-way-another-failed-incentive-scheme.

INDIA CELLULAR & ELECTRONICS ASSOCIATION (ICEA)  
India's Post-1991 Industrial and manufacturing Policy Landscape Page 6 of 11 
incentives and strong demand in global electronics markets, which encouraged firms to scale 
production in India.  
• Export growth: In the first three years, pharma sales under PLI crossed ₹ 2.66 lakh crore, including 
exports worth ₹ 1.70 lakh crore.11 Additionally, smartphones have emerged as India’s top exported 
commodity in CY 2025.12 
 
 
Figure 3: PLI Scheme, Investment & Employment Outcomes by Sector (2024).  
Source: Ministry of Commerce & Industry, Annual Reports 2023–24. 
 
Where It Fell Short 
• Under Utilisation of Allocated Budget: announced in 2020 with a committed outlay of ₹1.97 lakh 
crore over five years. However, between FY 2021–22 and FY 2023–24, actual spending and 
allocations remained below ₹12,000 crore, or 6% of the committed spending.13 
• Low domestic value-addition: Despite smartphone assembly growth, critical components 
(semiconductors, displays, camera modules) continue to be imported. Domestic value addition in 
electronics remains below 20%.14 India's electronics manufacturing growth has been driven largely 
by mobile phone assembly, but deeper value-chain segments such as semiconductor fabrication, 
display manufacturing, and advanced component ecosystems remain underdeveloped. 
 
11 Research Unit Press Information Bureau, “PLI Scheme: Powering India’s Industrial Renaissance: A Transformational Push for 
Manufacturing, Employment, and Global Competitiveness,” Press Information Bureau, August 24, 2025. 
https://www.pib.gov.in/PressNoteDetails.aspx?NoteId=155082&ModuleId=3&reg=3&lang=1  
12 PIB Delhi, “Domestic Value Addition in Electronics Manufacturing Has Improved Significantly Over the Years; Currently at 18%-
20%,” Press release, April 1, 2026. 
 https://www.pib.gov.in/PressReleseDetailm.aspx?PRID=2247771&reg=3&lang=2  
13 Dhar, “PLI: On the Way to Another Failed Incentive Scheme?”  
14 Press Information Bureau, “Domestic Value Addition in Electronics Manufacturing Rises to 18%-20%, Strengthening India’s 
Localisation Push,” India Brand Equity Foundation, April 2, 2026, https://www.ibef.org/news/domestic-value-addition-in-
electronics-manufacturing-rises-to-18-20-strengthening-india-s-localisation-push.

INDIA CELLULAR & ELECTRONICS ASSOCIATION (ICEA)  
India's Post-1991 Industrial and manufacturing Policy Landscape Page 7 of 11 
• Regulatory Complexity and Compliance Burden: PLI implementation has been hampered by multi-
layered approval processes across ministries, with no single-window coordination. Firms must meet 
annual sales thresholds, submit audited production data, and navigate scheme-specific eligibility 
criteria. ICEA has flagged that bottlenecks in component certification and customs clearance raise 
operational costs and delay investment decisions, disproportionately affecting smaller domestic 
firms that lack the compliance infrastructure of large multinationals.15 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
15 “THE CHALLENGES & OPPORTUNITIES IN INDIAN SEMICONDUCTOR INDUSTRY,” ICEA Knowledge Hub (India Cellular and 
Electronics Association), https://icea.org.in/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/0.1-The-challenges-and-opportunities-in-Indian-
Semiconductor-Industry.pdf.

INDIA CELLULAR & ELECTRONICS ASSOCIATION (ICEA)  
India's Post-1991 Industrial and manufacturing Policy Landscape Page 8 of 11 
3.  Comparative Lens: Vietnam's Export-Oriented Manufacturing Strategy 
Vietnam is widely regarded as one of the most successful examples of export-oriented industrial policy 
among developing economies. Following the Doi Moi16 economic reforms of 1986, the country gradually 
transitioned from an agriculture-dominated economy to a manufacturing-driven growth model integrated 
with global value chains (GVCs). In 1990, agriculture accounted for around 40% of Vietnam’s GDP while 
manufacturing contributed only about 12%. Over the following decades, structural transformation and 
industrial policy reforms significantly expanded manufacturing, which now contributes 25% of GDP, while 
agriculture’s share has declined to around 12%.17  
What Made It Effective? 
a)  Export Processing Zones with Functional Infrastructure: Vietnam established over 200 Export Processing 
Zones (EPZs) with dedicated, reliable infrastructure, power, roads, and port access, offering preferential tax 
regimes to attract manufacturing FDI.18 By contrast, India's equivalent Special Economic Zones (SEZs) 
suffered from policy reversals, including the imposition of Minimum Alternate Tax in 2011 and subsequent 
regulatory uncertainty, which eroded investor confidence and limited their effectiveness as export 
platforms. 
b)  Strategic Trade Agreement Architecture: Vietnam signed multiple free trade agreements including the 
EU-Vietnam Free Trade Agreement and the Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific 
Partnership (CPTPP), which significantly reduced tariffs and improved access to global markets. These 
agreements enabled manufacturers operating in Vietnam to export competitively to North America, Europe, 
and East Asia.19 India has stayed outside mega-regional trade frameworks, constraining the export 
competitiveness of firms manufacturing in India. 
c) Cost-Competitive Labour Force and Improving Infrastructure: Vietnam also leveraged its cost-
competitive labour force and improving infrastructure to attract global manufacturers relocating supply 
chains from higher-cost locations. Wages remain substantially lower than in China, while investments in 
ports, highways, and logistics have strengthened Vietnam’s export capacity.  
 
  
 
16 Felix Davis, “Vietnam Rising: Why Multinational Corporations Should Invest in Southeast Asia’s Fastest-Growing Economy,” 
October 17, 2025, https://doi.org/10.58445/rars.3245.  
17 Davis, “Vietnam Rising: Why Multinational Corporations Should Invest in Southeast Asia’s Fastest-Growing Economy.” 
18 Davis, “Vietnam Rising: Why Multinational Corporations Should Invest in Southeast Asia’s Fastest-Growing Economy.” 
19 Davis, “Vietnam Rising: Why Multinational Corporations Should Invest in Southeast Asia’s Fastest-Growing Economy.”

INDIA CELLULAR & ELECTRONICS ASSOCIATION (ICEA)  
India's Post-1991 Industrial and manufacturing Policy Landscape Page 9 of 11 
Conclusion 
India’s manufacturing stagnation reflects deep structural constraints, including services-led growth, a 
fragmented industrial base, weak integration into GVCs, and policy coordination gaps. These challenges are 
reinforced by structural competitiveness constraints such as high infrastructure costs, policy distortions in 
trade and industrial policy, and limited investments in skills and technology. While PLI and Make in India 
have generated measurable gains in FDI and electronics exports, structural transformation remains 
incomplete. Vietnam’s experience demonstrates that sustained industrial growth requires coherent 
alignment of trade policy, export infrastructure, and supply chain deepening, an approach India’s 
industrial policy must now prioritise.

INDIA CELLULAR & ELECTRONICS ASSOCIATION (ICEA)  
India's Post-1991 Industrial and manufacturing Policy Landscape Page 10 of 11

AI Tool Disclosure 
AI-assisted research tools were used to support the literature review and source discovery for this 
assignment. Connected Papers, Consensus AI and Google Scholars were used to identify relevant academic 
literature and highly cited research on industrial policy, manufacturing, and global value chains. Mendeley 
was used for organising, managing, and reading research papers and reports referenced in the analysis. 
Additionally, ChatGPT and QuillBot were occasionally used to clarify doubts regarding the flow and structuring 
of the writing. However, the analysis, interpretation of sources, and final arguments presented in the 
assignment were developed independently.

1 
India’s Startup Ecosystem After DeepSeek: post the release of DeepSeek  The release of DeepSeek-R1 in January 2025 became an important turning point in the global artificial intelligence (AI) sector and had noticeable effects on India’s startup ecosystem. For nearly two years after the launch of large language models (LLMs) by companies like OpenAI and Google, most Indian AI startups were working mainly at the application layer. This means that startups were building tools such as chatbots, productivity software, marketing tools, or industry-specific assistants by using existing foundation models developed by foreign companies through API access. However, this approach led to high operational costs, limited long-term competitive advantage, and dependence on external companies for pricing and policies1.  The emergence of DeepSeek changed this trend by showing that high-performing AI reasoning models could be built at a much lower cost than previously believed. DeepSeek released performance results that were comparable to leading proprietary AI models, while reportedly spending less than $6 million on training2. This changed the common assumption that only large, well-funded firms could develop advanced AI models. For Indian startups, which often face funding limitations and lack access to large-scale computing infrastructure like GPU clusters, this significantly reduced the barriers to entering the model development layer of AI.  As a result, Indian startups have started moving beyond just building applications and are now focusing more on infrastructure, tools, and domain-specific foundation models. Many Indian startups are experimenting with open-weight models, fine-tuning techniques, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and low-rank adaptation (LoRA) to build AI systems suited for Indian needs3. These include systems for Indic-language communication, agricultural advisory services, legal document analysis, climate-risk assessment, and health sector4.  This shift has also increased interest in building more compute-efficient solutions. Indian AI firms are working on methods such as model compression, quantization, edge deployment, and inference optimization to make AI systems work effectively within hardware limitations. Unlike earlier approaches that focused on building larger models, the post-DeepSeek approach emphasizes building efficient models that perform well with fewer resources. This aligns with India’s traditional strength in cost-effective technological innovation5.  Investment trends are also beginning to reflect this change. Venture capital firms are showing greater interest in startups working on AI infrastructure such as middleware platforms, orchestration tools,  1 NASSCOM (2024), AI Adoption Index 2.0: Tracking India’s Sectoral Progress in AI Adoption (2024). https://nasscom.in/knowledge-center/publications/ai-adoption-index-20-tracking-indias-sectoral-progress-ai-adoption

Artificial intelligence (AI) startups in health sector in India: challenges and regulation in India. In Algorithms for intelligent systems (pp. 203–215). https://doi.org/10.1007/978-981-19-2065-3_24  5 Chauhan, A., & Rao, V. (2025, January 30). DeepSeek effect: We can do a Mangalyaan in AI, say India’s top AI experts. The Indian Express. https://indianexpress.com/article/technology/deepseek-r1-india-mangalyaan-ai-9805659/

2 
model monitoring systems, synthetic data generation, and domain-specific model training6. This suggests that India’s AI startup ecosystem is slowly shifting from simply using AI technology to building core AI capabilities.  From a policy perspective, this shift is important for India’s digital independence. Developing indigenous AI models can reduce reliance on foreign technology providers and support AI deployment in public services across India’s diverse linguistic and socio-economic contexts. However, sustaining this progress will require government support in the form of computing infrastructure, access to open datasets, and clear regulatory guidelines for training AI models on public data.  DeepSeek has not only introduced a new AI model but has also changed how startups think about AI development. By demonstrating that advanced AI capabilities can be built at lower costs, it has encouraged Indian startups to move from building AI-based applications to developing AI infrastructure and core technologies.

Briefing Note: CERC (Carbon Credit Certificates) Regulations, 2026
 
1 
TL; DR 
● On February 27, 2026, the Central Electricity Regulatory Commission (CERC) notified the 
Carbon Credit Certificate (CCC) trading regulations, creating India’s first formal regulatory 
framework for carbon credit exchange under the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS), 
2023. 
● The regulations establish Power Exchanges as the primary platform for buying and selling 
CCCs between Obligated and Non-Obligated entities. 
● The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) is designated as the Administrator, while the Grid 
Controller of India will function as the Registry, for CCC trading. 
● Price discovery will occur on Power Exchanges, bounded by a floor price and a forbearance 
price as approved by CERC. 
● CERC retains oversight powers to intervene in cases of abnormal price volatility and to impose 
penalties for non-compliance. 
 
Context 
On February 27, 2026, the Central Electricity Regulatory Commission (CERC) notified the CERC 
(Terms and Conditions for Purchase and Sale of Carbon Credit Certificates) Regulations, 2026. These 
regulations operationalise the trading infrastructure for the Indian Carbon Market (ICM), established 
under the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS), 2023, issued by the Ministry of Power. The CERC 
framework specifies the roles of key institutions, market structure, pricing boundaries, transaction 
rules, and oversight mechanisms for the exchange of Carbon Credit Certificates (CCCs). 
The regulations build on an evolving institutional landscape for carbon markets in India. In September 
2025, the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change constituted the National Designated 
Authority to implement Article 6 of the Paris Agreement. This aims to establish mechanisms for 
carbon markets, signalling India’s intent to align its domestic carbon market with international 
frameworks. The CERC regulations now provide the market framework needed to support the 
broader architecture. 
Key Provisions  
● Institutional Architecture: The regulations create a three-institution structure. The BEE 
serves as the Administrator, formulating transaction procedures, disseminating market 
information, monitoring CCC exchange transparency, and liaising with Power Exchanges and 
the Registry. The Grid Controller of India functions as the Registry, maintaining CCC 
accounts, tracking transactions, and flagging defaulters. CERC exercises overall market 
oversight and regulatory supervision, assisted by the BEE, in accordance with the Central 
Electricity Regulatory Commission (Power Market) Regulations, 2021. 
● Market Structure and Trading Segments: The regulations create two distinct market 
segments for CCC trading: a Compliance Market for Obligated Entities, and an Offset 
Market for Non-Obligated Entities. Trading will occur primarily on CERC-approved Power 
Exchanges. These are electronic trading platforms where electricity buyers and sellers come 
together to buy and sell power, such as the Indian Energy Exchange (IEX) and the Power 
Exchange India Limited (PXIL). Transactions will occur on a monthly basis or as otherwise 
approved by the Commission.

Briefing Note: CERC (Carbon Credit Certificates) Regulations, 2026
 
2 
● Certificate Value and Pricing: Each CCC represents the reduction, removal, or 
avoidance of 1 tonne of CO₂ equivalent (tCO2e), as specified under the CCTS, 
with market prices discovered at the respective Power Exchange through CERC-
approved mechanisms. Under the Compliance Mechanism, all transactions must 
occur within a Floor Price (minimum) and a Forbearance Price (maximum), both 
approved by CERC on a proposal from BEE. CERC retains the power to issue 
directions to Power Exchanges, permitted entities, or the Registry in cases of 
abnormal price movements, sudden volatility, or unusually high or low trading 
volumes. 
● Transaction Rules and Default Provisions: Entities may place sale bids only up to the 
number of CCCs available in their Registry account. Bids exceeding the available balance will 
be treated as invalid. Entities with more than three such defaults in a quarter will be barred 
from CCC trading for six months and may face penalties under the Energy Conservation Act, 
2001. The Registry will publish a monthly list of defaulting entities. 
● Banking, Surrender and Fees: Banking and surrender rules for CCCs will follow the 
Detailed Procedures for Compliance and Offset Mechanisms under the CCTS. Fees and 
charges payable by Obligated and Non-Obligated entities to the Registry will be determined 
by CERC in consultation with BEE from time to time. 
Implications for Market Participants 
● Obligated Entities under the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS), 2023 must procure and 
surrender CCCs to meet emissions targets, with non-compliance attracting penalties under the 
Energy Conservation Act, 2001. 
● Entities that reduce emissions beyond their targets can sell surplus certificates on Power 
Exchanges, creating a potential revenue stream. 
● Entities seeking to trade CCCs must register with approved Power Exchanges such as the 
Indian Energy Exchange (IEX) and the Power Exchange India Limited (PXIL). They must 
maintain sufficient certificate balances in the Registry maintained by the Grid Controller of 
India. Firms may need to develop internal monitoring, compliance tracking, and trading 
capabilities. 
● Detailed transaction procedures, including registration requirements, transfer rules, and 
interface protocols, will be issued by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency in the coming months.

●  The  Ministry  of  Power  has  introduced  the  Draft  National  Electricity  Policy  (NEP),  2026  
for
 
public

The
 
policy
 
proposes

for
 
a

affordable,

and
 
environmentally
 
sustainable
 
power
 
sector
 
aligned
 
with
 
India’s
 
2047
 
energy
 
goals.
 ●  It  replaces  the  2005  policy  and  responds  to  new  challenges  such  as  financial  stress  of  
DISCOMs,
 
large-scale
 
renewable
 
integration,
 
and
 
energy
 
transition.
 ●  The  policy  emphasises  cost-reflective  tariffs,  reduction  of  cross-subsidies,  and  promotion  
of

especially
 
in
 
power
 
supply
 
and
 
markets.
 ●  It  prioritises  non-fossil  generation,  energy  storage,  grid  resilience,  and  consumer-centric  
services.
 ●  Strong  focus  is  also  placed  on  digitalisation,  smart  infrastructure,  cybersecurity,  and  data  
sharing.
 ●  For  large  consumers,  the  policy  could  enable  greater  access  to  competitively  priced  clean  
power
 
and

the

environment
 
for

energy
 
projects
 
in
 
India.
 
Introduction  On  January  20,  2026,  The  Ministry  of  Power  introduced  the  Draft  National  Electricity  Policy  (NEP),  2026 for  stakeholder  consultation.  It  replaces  the  National  Electricity  Policy,  2005,  which  focused  on  universal  electrification  and  capacity  expansion.  NEP  2026  responds  to  India’s  
transformed
 
power
 
sector
 
where
 
access
 
has
 
largely
 
been
 
achieved,
 
but
 
financial
 
viability,
 
competitiveness,
 
renewable
 
integration
 
and
 
grid
 
resilience
 
remain
 
key
 
challenges.
 
The
 
policy
 
proposes
 
a
 
strategic
 
framework
 
to
 
ensure
 
reliable
 
24x7
 
power,
 
support
 
India’s
 
energy
 
transition
 
and
 
climate
 
commitments,
 
strengthen
 
competition
 
and
 
power
 
markets,
 
improve
 
the
 
financial
 
sustainability
 
of
 
utilities,
 
and
 
deliver
 
consumer-centric
 
services.
 
Feedback
 
on
 
the
 
draft
 
can
 
be
 shared  with  the  Ministry until  February  19,  2026.  
Policy  Objectives  ●  Financial  turn  around  and  commercial  viability  of  the  electricity  sector.  ●  Ensuring  adequate  availability  of  power  with  reliable  and  quality  supply,  while  meeting  peak  demand  and  energy  requirements.  ●  Supply  of  electricity  at  competitive  prices.  ●  Increase  non-fossil  capacity  to  meet  India’s  NDC  targets.  ●  Promote  competition  in  supply  of  electricity.  ●  Increase  per  capita  electricity  consumption  to  2,000  kWh  by  2030  and  over  4,000  kWh  by  2047.  ●  Enable  large-scale  renewable  integration  and  address  climate  and  cyber  risks.  ●  Improve  service  quality  and  demand-side  participation.  ●  Faster  resolution  to  reduce  financial  burden  on  consumers.  
Features  of  the  Policy  ●  Resource  adequacy  and  planning:  NEP  2026  shifts  power  planning  from  a  fully  
centralised
 
model
 
to
 
a
 
more
 
decentralised
 
one.
 
DISCOMs
 
and
 
State
 
Load
 
Despatch
 
Centres
 
(SLDCs)
 
will
 
now
 
prepare
 
their
 
own
 
resource
 
adequacy
 
plans
 
based
 
on
 
local
 
demand,
 
network
 
conditions
 
and
 
reliability
 
needs.
 
These
 
plans
 
will
 
be
 
aligned
 
with
 
a
 
national
 
framework
 
issued
 
by
 
the
 
Central
 
Electricity
 
Authority
 
(CEA),
 
ensuring
 
that
 
capacity
 
is
 
added
 
on
 
time,
 
at
 
the
 
lowest
 
cost,
 
and
 
with
 
system
 
reliability
 
in
 
mind.

energy

Early  Reactions  Market  analysts  have  described the  Draft  NEP  2026  as  an  effort  to  move  away  from  a  rigid,  coal-heavy  system  towards  a  more  flexible,  renewable  and  market-driven  power  sector.  They  
argue
 
that
 
long-term
 
contracts,
 
poor
 
grid
 
incentives
 
and
 
weak
 
state-level
 
networks
 
have
 
made
 
DISCOMs
 
inefficient,
 
and
 
that
 
tools
 
such
 
as
 
VPPAs,
 
competitive
 
transmission
 
bidding
 
and
 
independent
 
grid
 
operators
 
could
 
help
 
fix
 
these
 
problems.
 
However,
 
they
 
warn
 
that
 
the
 
reforms
 
will
 
only
 
succeed
 
if
 
the
 
rules
 
and
 
institutions
 
change
 
fast
 
enough
 
to
 
support
 
a
 
cleaner,
 
more
 
dynamic
 
grid.
 
A  key  financial  reform  in  the  draft  policy  requires  tariffs  to  be  automatically  revised  each  year  
from
 
FY
 
2026-27
 
if
 
state
 
regulators
 
do
 
not
 
issue
 
a
 
tariff
 
order.
 
While
 
the
 
government
 
says
 
this
 will  bring  pricing  discipline  and  predictability,  consumer  groups  worry it  could  result  in  regular  electricity  price  hikes.  
The  All  India  Power  Engineers  Federation  (AIPEF)  has  opposed the  policy,  calling  it  pro-privatisation  and  harmful  to  consumers,  farmers  and  power  sector  workers.  It  argues  that  
private
 
companies
 
will
 
use
 
public
 
networks
 
while
 
shifting
 
losses
 
and
 
service
 
obligations
 
onto
 
state
 
DISCOMs,
 
which
 
could
 
push
 
tariffs
 
higher.
 
AIPEF
 
also
 
warns
 
that
 
private
 
suppliers
 
may
 
focus
 
only
 
on
 
profitable
 
urban
 
areas,
 
leaving
 
rural
 
and
 
agricultural
 
consumers
 
underserved.
